name: Gary Gaulin tracksite, Holyoke
short_description: Historic tracksite featuring fossil footprints in Jurassic-era rocks. A unique destination for paleontology enthusiasts and rockhounds interested in Connecticut Valley geology.
description:
The Gary Gaulin tracksite near Holyoke preserves evidence of ancient life from the Jurassic period, approximately 200 million years ago. The site sits within the Connecticut Valley, a major paleontological zone that has yielded some of North America's most significant dinosaur trackways and fossil deposits. Visitors can observe and study the preserved tracks pressed into reddish sandstone and mudstone layers that form the local bedrock.

The flat terrain at 426 feet elevation makes this site easily accessible for families and casual rockhounds. The geological formations here record a time when the Connecticut Valley contained a large, shallow lake surrounded by the ancient supercontinent. The sediments that buried and preserved these tracks are now exposed through natural erosion and roadcuts.

This is primarily a viewing and documentation site rather than a heavy collection area. The scientific value of the trackways makes preservation important, and visitors are encouraged to photograph and study the specimens in place. Rock and mineral enthusiasts can find quartz and feldspar specimens in the surrounding weathered outcrops.

The site offers insight into how paleontologists identify and interpret trace fossils, and it connects to the broader story of the Connecticut Valley's role in understanding dinosaur behavior and ecosystems. Access is generally available, though specific regulations may vary.
history: The Connecticut Valley's Jurassic rocks were deposited in a large rift basin during the early Jurassic period, between 200 and 190 million years ago. Dinosaurs and other organisms that walked along the shores of this ancient lake left their footprints in soft mud, which hardened into the red sandstones and siltstones visible today. The Gary Gaulin tracksite is one of hundreds of similar sites throughout the Connecticut Valley that have made the region a world-class source for understanding dinosaur paleontology.
tips: Look carefully at the reddish-brown sandstone and mudstone layers for three-toed and other trackway impressions. Bring a magnifying glass to examine details of the track morphology and appreciate how paleontologists distinguish species. Photograph any unusual patterns you find, as this site is best respected through observation and documentation rather than removal. Wear sturdy footwear with good grip, as rocks can be slippery after rain and the terrain is uneven in places.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at 426 feet elevation. The site features exposed sandstone and mudstone outcrops with natural reddish-brown coloration. Vegetation includes typical New England deciduous and mixed forest. Walking distance from parking is minimal to moderate. Footing is generally stable on established paths, but rocks can be uneven and slippery when wet. The bedrock is well-exposed and accessible without heavy scrambling.
best_season: May - September. Late spring through early fall offers the most pleasant weather for outdoor exploration. Summer provides maximum daylight hours for detailed study and photography. Early spring and fall are also good, though mornings may be cooler and footing can be slippery from leaf litter and moisture.
written_directions: From central Holyoke, head north on Bridge Street or follow local directional signage toward the town of Holyoke proper. The tracksite is located within Holyoke proper near Route 141. Use the GPS coordinates 42.197227, -72.644188 with a smartphone GPS or mapping app for precise navigation. This is a short drive from downtown Holyoke, approximately 1-2 miles. Parking is typically available near the site entrance; look for marked pullouts or small parking areas associated with the tracksite.
road_conditions: Paved local roads lead to the site. Final approach may be on a gravel pullout or unpaved parking area. Normal passenger vehicle clearance is sufficient.
parking_notes: Parking is available at or very near the tracksite. Space is typically limited to a small pullout or informal parking area. Arrive early during peak season to secure parking.
collection_rules: The Gary Gaulin tracksite is a scientifically significant paleontological site. Collection of fossil specimens is not permitted. Visitors may observe, study, and photograph trackways in place. Removal of rock or sediment containing fossils is prohibited to preserve the site for research and education. Casual collection of loose quartz and feldspar specimens from surrounding weathered material is permissible, but focus on documentation of the trackways themselves.
rules:
No collection of fossil specimens
Photography and observation permitted
No removal of tracked rock layers
Respect site preservation for future study
Follow all posted signage and directions
Casual collection of loose quartz/feldspar from weathered material only
nearest_services: Holyoke, MA (immediately adjacent, 1 mile). Full services including gas stations, restaurants, and retail shops available throughout Holyoke.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most visitors. Paved roads and unpaved parking area lead directly to the site. Short walking distance required. Uneven rocky surfaces and potentially slippery footing after rain. No wheelchair accessibility reported. Sturdy footwear with good grip is recommended. Generally suitable for families with children and casual rockhounders.

faq.question: What can you find at the Gary Gaulin tracksite in Holyoke, Massachusetts?
faq.answer: The Gary Gaulin tracksite is primarily known for preserved dinosaur footprints and trace fossils in reddish Jurassic-era sandstone and mudstone. You will also find quartz and feldspar specimens in weathered outcrops surrounding the trackways. This is a viewing site focused on paleontology rather than heavy gem collection, making it ideal for rockhounds interested in fossils and Connecticut Valley geology.

faq.question: Is there a fee to visit the Gary Gaulin tracksite, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: There is no admission fee or permit required to visit the Gary Gaulin tracksite. Access is open to the public as a free site near Holyoke. However, fossil specimen collection is prohibited to preserve the site's scientific value. You may observe, photograph, and study the trackways in place, and casually collect loose quartz and feldspar from weathered material.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit the Gary Gaulin tracksite?
faq.answer: The best season to visit is May through September, when weather is mild and daylight hours are long for detailed study and photography. Late spring and early fall are also excellent, though mornings may be cooler. Avoid winter when footing can be icy or slippery, and early spring when mud and wet leaf litter make surfaces treacherous.

faq.question: Can beginners and families visit the Gary Gaulin tracksite for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, the Gary Gaulin tracksite is highly beginner and family-friendly. The flat terrain at 426 feet elevation requires minimal hiking, and the parking area is immediately accessible. Children ages 6 and up can enjoy observing and photographing the ancient trackways. The educational value of learning about Jurassic dinosaurs makes it an excellent field trip destination for families.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to the Gary Gaulin tracksite?
faq.answer: Bring a magnifying glass or hand lens to examine track morphology and fossil details. A camera or smartphone for photography is essential to document your observations. Sturdy hiking boots with good grip are important, as rocks can be uneven and slippery when wet. Sunscreen, water, and weather-appropriate clothing are recommended. A field notebook can help you record your findings and observations.

faq.question: Is the Gary Gaulin tracksite in Holyoke good for rockhounding quartz and feldspar?
faq.answer: While the site is primarily valued for its fossil trackways, you can find quartz and feldspar specimens in weathered sandstone and mudstone outcrops surrounding the main tracksite. The reddish Jurassic-era rocks weather to reveal these common silicate minerals. Focus on the edges of the main rock exposures and loose material rather than the tracked specimens themselves, which must be preserved in place.

meta_title: Gary Gaulin Tracksite Holyoke - Jurassic Fossils MA
meta_description: Explore dinosaur footprints and trace fossils at the Gary Gaulin tracksite near Holyoke, MA. Free public access, beginner-friendly Jurassic geology site.
